patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a cooperative 17-year-old who speaks clearly and answers directly. Start with: “I get tired and weak really fast, especially in my face and arms, and it gets worse as the day goes on.” Early in the visit, volunteer: eyelids droop by afternoon; arms feel like jelly after brushing hair/holding things up; speech gets slurred after talking; you can do something at first but fade with repetition; resting helps. If asked about eating/swallowing/breathing, share: chewing tires with tougher foods; occasional coughing/choking with water; short of breath when sick; worse with fatigue, stress, heat, poor sleep; symptoms since childhood; head feels heavy to hold up. Do not volunteer: avoiding long calls/socializing, needing breaks while eating, tripping on stairs late day, unrefreshing sleep/morning headaches, small size/poor weight gain—only mention if asked about daily impact or sleep. Withhold bedwetting/urine leakage and dating worries unless specifically asked about bladder/sexual concerns; then admit, embarrassed. Express misconceptions: maybe anxiety, low iron/vitamins, or “just migraines.” If clinician is empathetic, relax, thank them, and share more details. Emphasize cost worries about genetic testing/specialists, long-term meds/PT, and fear accommodations could hurt college/jobs/insurance.

diagnosis.primary: congenital myasthenic syndromes with glycosylation defect

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital myasthenic syndrome (CMS) due to glycosylation defect (e.g., GFPT1/DPAGT1-related CMS)
acceptable_alternatives: Congenital myasthenic syndrome (CMS), glycosylation pathway subtype
required_evidence: Chronic fluctuating fatigable weakness beginning in infancy/childhood (often limb-girdle and/or ocular/bulbar involvement) without sensory loss
Normal cognition and no upper motor neuron signs; symptoms worsen with exertion/illness
Electrophysiology consistent with neuromuscular junction transmission defect (decrement on low-frequency RNS and/or increased jitter on SFEMG)
No acetylcholine receptor (AChR) or MuSK autoantibodies (supports non-autoimmune etiology)
Genetic confirmation of a glycosylation-pathway CMS (e.g., GFPT1, DPAGT1, GMPPB) or strong phenotype consistent with this subtype
Clinical improvement with appropriate CMS-directed therapy (e.g., pyridostigmine and/or 3,4-diaminopyridine), and avoidance of known worsening drugs (e.g., macrolides)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically within reference range (no specific abnormality expected for CMS)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically within reference range unless intercurrent infection
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - Bicarbon: Typically within reference range; may be decreased only if acute hyperventilation/respiratory distress is present

medications: Pyridostigmine 30 mg PO every 6 hours while awake (titrate to effect/tolerability; pediatric dosing 0.5-1 mg/kg/dose q4-6h if applicable)
Amifampridine (3,4-diaminopyridine) 10 mg PO three times daily (titrate gradually; typical max 80 mg/day; consider ECG monitoring for QT risk)
Albuterol (salbutamol) 2 mg PO three times daily (alternative/adjunct in some CMS subtypes; monitor heart rate and tremor)
non_pharmacological: Avoid medications that can worsen neuromuscular transmission, especially macrolides (e.g., azithromycin), fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides, magnesium-containing products, and neuromuscular blockers when possible
Energy conservation strategies and paced activity; referral to PT/OT for strengthening within tolerance and fall-risk mitigation
Respiratory monitoring plan if bulbar/respiratory involvement (peak flow/overnight oximetry as indicated) and emergency plan for worsening dyspnea
Genetic counseling and family screening as appropriate once genotype confirmed
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neuromuscular specialist follow-up in 2-4 weeks for response and titration; earlier (within 48-72 hours) if worsening weakness, dysphagia, or shortness of breath

must_include: Condition is an inherited problem of neuromuscular transmission (not autoimmune myasthenia gravis)
Weakness is typically fatigable and can fluctuate; infections/exertion can worsen symptoms
Glycosylation-defect CMS is a specific genetic subtype; confirmatory testing may include EMG and genetic testing
Goal is to improve neuromuscular signal transmission and reduce fatigable weakness
How and when to take pyridostigmine (and potential side effects: abdominal cramps/diarrhea, increased secretions, bradycardia)
Rationale and titration plan for amifampridine (and risks: tingling, seizures at high doses, QT concerns) and when to seek help
Some subtypes respond to beta-agonists (albuterol); monitoring for palpitations/tremor
Red flags requiring urgent care: worsening shortness of breath, inability to swallow/manage secretions, rapidly progressive weakness
Medication safety: avoid azithromycin/macrolides due to prior worsened weakness/shortness of breath; document penicillin allergy (urticarial rash)
Discuss expectations: improvement may require dose adjustments and may not be immediate; importance of follow-up
Avoid triggers and pacing strategies (rest breaks, avoid overheating, manage infections early)
Carry a medication/allergy list and inform clinicians/anesthesiology about CMS and drug sensitivities
Plan for school/work accommodations and physical therapy as needed

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for fatigable weakness pattern, bulbar/respiratory symptoms, triggers (infection/exertion/heat), prior medication responses, and current meds/allergies to guide safe treatment.

Initiate confirmatory evaluation appropriate for suspected CMS (e.g., EMG with repetitive nerve stimulation/single-fiber EMG, genetic testing panel for CMS/glycosylation pathway genes) and baseline safety tests as needed for planned therapies.
Review results to support diagnosis of congenital (genetic) neuromuscular transmission disorder and exclude common mimics where relevant.
State diagnosis as congenital myasthenic syndrome with glycosylation defect and distinguish from autoimmune myasthenia gravis; document fluctuating fatigable weakness and triggers.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, expected course/trigger management, red flags, and treatment options including pyridostigmine, amifampridine, and possible beta-agonists with monitoring.

Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm and document penicillin allergy (urticarial rash) and prior macrolide-associated worsening; ensure planned meds do not contain allergens/excipients of concern.
Critical safety check before prescribing: screen current meds/supplements for interactions (e.g., QT-prolonging agents with amifampridine; other agents that can worsen neuromuscular transmission).

Critical safety check before prescribing: assess contraindications such as seizure history (amifampridine), significant arrhythmia/QT issues, severe asthma/thyrotoxicosis (beta-agonists), bradyarrhythmia (pyridostigmine).

Initiate evidence-based symptomatic therapy with clear dosing/titration, side effects, and when-to-seek-care instructions; avoid contraindicated antibiotics (macrolides) and document medication sensitivities.
Arrange close follow-up for titration, monitoring efficacy/adverse effects, and to review genetic/EMG results; provide contingency plan for deterioration.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 72-year-old retired auto mechanic (now part-time hardware store greeter). You are anxious but speak clearly and directly.

Start with: “For the past few months I’ve been getting really unsteady on my feet, like I’m drunk even when I haven’t had anything to drink.” Volunteer early: bumping into things/balance off, more fatigue with chores, more pressure-like headaches, and occasional blurry vision (worse when tired).

If asked about bleeding/neurologic/cardiopulmonary/constitutional/urinary symptoms, disclose: a few nosebleeds and gums bleed when brushing; tingling/numb hands/feet; short of breath on stairs; ankle/leg swelling by evening; mild unintentional weight loss, low appetite; intermittent night sweats.

Do not volunteer unless specifically asked: cold-induced finger color change/pain, brief confusion “foggy” episodes, early satiety, foamy urine and less urine some days.

Resist discussing erectile problems/avoiding sex unless asked directly; then admit embarrassment.

Express misconceptions: worry about tick-related brain infection, “blood sugar” when skipping meals, or connective-tissue issues.

Ask about costs, Medicare coverage, and minimizing tests/PT. If clinician shows empathy, you relax, thank them, and become more forthcoming.

confounders: Chronic kidney disease with uremic symptoms can contribute to gait instability and neuropathy-like complaints

acceptable_alternatives: Lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma with IgM monoclonal gammopathy

required_evidence: Ataxia consistent with IgM-related hyperviscosity or neuropathy
Elevated serum total protein with relatively low/normal albumin (suggesting increased globulins)
Elevated ESR (often markedly increased with high immunoglobulin levels)
Cytopenias on CBC (anemia and/or thrombocytopenia) consistent with marrow involvement

Confirmatory testing to be pursued: serum protein electrophoresis/immunofixation showing IgM monoclonal protein, quantitative IgM level, serum viscosity if symptomatic, bone marrow biopsy showing lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma (often MYD88 L265P mutation)

Bendamustine 90 mg/m2 IV days 1-2 every 28 days x 4-6 cycles (in combination with rituximab for symptomatic disease, per hematology/oncology)
Alternative systemic option (if chemoimmunotherapy not suitable): Ibrutinib 420 mg PO daily (or zanubrutinib 160 mg PO BID) per hematology/oncology
non_pharmacological: Urgent hematology/oncology referral for confirmation (SPEP/IFE, quantitative IgM, serum viscosity, bone marrow biopsy) and initiation of disease-directed therapy
Assess for hyperviscosity and neurologic red flags; if acute/worsening ataxia, consider ED evaluation and neuroimaging to exclude stroke/structural causes while treating suspected hyperviscosity
Avoid dehydration; encourage adequate oral hydration unless contraindicated
Avoid red blood cell transfusion unless absolutely necessary in active hyperviscosity (can worsen viscosity); if needed, coordinate with hematology and consider plasma exchange first
Fall-risk precautions and physical therapy evaluation for gait instability/ataxia
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Same day urgent evaluation if ataxia is acute/progressive or any hyperviscosity features; otherwise hematology/oncology follow-up within 1 week with confirmatory testing and treatment initiation plan

key_points: Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia is a bone-marrow lymphoma that makes excess IgM protein
High IgM can thicken blood (hyperviscosity) and/or affect nerves, potentially causing ataxia
Need for confirmatory tests (SPEP/IFE, quantitative IgM, serum viscosity, bone marrow biopsy) even if initial labs suggest the pattern
If hyperviscosity is suspected, plasma exchange can rapidly relieve neurologic symptoms and is urgent
Longer-term control requires lymphoma-directed therapy (e.g., rituximab-based therapy or BTK inhibitor) guided by hematology
Rituximab can transiently increase IgM (IgM flare); this is why timing with plasma exchange/combination therapy matters
Red-flag symptoms requiring emergency care: worsening confusion, new focal weakness, vision changes, severe headache, bleeding, chest pain, shortness of breath
Discuss expected side effects and infection/bleeding risks from therapy and when to call
Confirm penicillin allergy history and clarify reaction (urticarial rash) for future antibiotic planning
Hydration and avoiding factors that worsen viscosity (dehydration)
Fall-prevention strategies given ataxia (assistive devices, home safety)
Importance of keeping follow-up and monitoring labs/symptoms during therapy

Critical safety check prior to prescribing; ensure no contraindications/precautions that require modification or monitoring (e.g., untreated systemic infection without appropriate management, severe uncontrolled diabetes, active GI bleed risk, psychosis) while recognizing replacement-dose steroids are generally indicated when adrenal insufficiency is present.

Initiate physiologic glucocorticoid replacement (e.g., hydrocortisone split dosing) and provide an emergency injectable steroid plan; avoid fludrocortisone unless evidence of primary AI; ensure thyroid hormone is not started/increased before steroid replacement if central hypothyroidism is present.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (secondary vs primary), treatment schedule and sick-day rules, adverse effects vs physiologic replacement, adrenal crisis warning signs, and lifestyle preparedness (medical alert, extra meds, injection kit, hydration).

Arrange timely follow-up for symptom response, dose titration, education reinforcement, and evaluation/management of other pituitary axes (including timing of testosterone adjustments after adrenal replacement is stabilized).
Close encounter after ensuring plan, red flags, and follow-up are clear.

required_evidence: Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia suggested by anemia with elevated LDH, low haptoglobin, and elevated indirect bilirubin

Reticulocytosis consistent with hemolysis
Clinical context compatible with typical HUS (often follows diarrheal illness; absence of alternative causes of MAHA such as TTP/aHUS is supportive)

Packed red blood cells 10-15 mL/kg IV once, repeat as needed for symptomatic anemia or hemodynamic compromise
Acetaminophen 10-15 mg/kg PO/IV every 4-6 hours as needed for pain/fever (max 60 mg/kg/day or 3 g/day, whichever lower)
non_pharmacological: Supportive care is primary: close monitoring of vitals, strict intake/output, daily weights
Avoid antibiotics and antimotility agents when STEC suspected/typical HUS (may worsen toxin-mediated disease)
Avoid nephrotoxins (e.g., NSAIDs, IV contrast, aminoglycosides) and adjust all medication dosing for renal function

Platelet transfusion only for significant bleeding or before urgent invasive procedures (not for isolated thrombocytopenia without bleeding)

Describe possible need for dialysis if kidney function worsens and outline triggers for escalation

Set expectations about hospital course and monitoring frequency

Hydration guidance tailored to urine output/clinical status (avoid overhydration if oliguric)
Avoid NSAIDs and other kidney-stressing medications unless specifically advised

Open with: “I’ve been really wiped out, and my eyes look kind of yellow.” Early in the visit, volunteer: 2 weeks of severe fatigue, yellow eyes/skin, tea-colored urine, shortness of breath on stairs, and occasional racing heart.

Do not mention unless specifically asked: small firm bumps near your elbow, dry gritty eyes needing artificial tears, occasional sharp chest twinge with deep breath that improves with rest, and mild unintentional weight loss/low appetite. Resist sharing unless strong rapport: avoiding intimacy/low interest and increased nightly alcohol to cope; if asked gently, admit you’re worried about judgment. Express misconceptions: maybe carpal tunnel from typing, worry about Lyme from a hike, or “just aging/osteoarthritis like my mom.” Emphasize cost/work concerns and preference for minimal tests. If the clinician is empathetic, you relax, answer more fully, and ask about affordable options.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a cooperative, clear-speaking 52-year-old man. Open the visit by saying: “I got really short of breath all of a sudden and it keeps getting worse.” Also volunteer: dry cough for a few days, feeling feverish/chilled, extreme fatigue, and chest tightness with deep breaths.

If asked about severity/function: say you can’t speak full sentences without pausing, get winded walking to the bathroom, and you’re breathing faster even at rest. If asked about sputum: mostly none, maybe a little clear. If asked about color/oxygen: mention lips/fingertips looked bluish during bad episodes and home oxygen isn’t helping much (only if asked). If asked about sleep/position: admit needing more pillows because lying flat worsens breathing. If asked about heart/urine: racing heart during episodes and peeing less.

Withhold vaping THC unless directly asked about smoking/vaping/drugs; then admit you were worried about being judged.

Express misconceptions: you thought flu/COVID; worried about blood clot or heart.

If clinician shows empathy, thank them and become more forthcoming.

Bring up cost/insurance/work concerns if treatment or follow-up is discussed.

medications: Nintedanib 150 mg PO twice daily
Sildenafil 20 mg PO three times daily
Furosemide 20 mg PO daily
Oxygen therapy 4 L/min via nasal cannula continuous
confounders: Recent viral-like upper respiratory illness with fever and leukocytosis (mimics infectious pneumonia)
Chronic pulmonary hypertension with elevated BNP and baseline exertional dyspnea (can mimic cardiogenic pulmonary edema/right heart strain)
Nintedanib-associated transaminitis (elevated AST/ALT complicates systemic inflammatory interpretation)
Elevated D-dimer in acute inflammatory lung injury (can prompt concern for pulmonary embolism)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute interstitial pneumonia (AIP; idiopathic diffuse alveolar damage/ARDS pattern)
acceptable_alternatives: Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) due to unidentified trigger
Acute exacerbation of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (AE-IPF) with diffuse alveolar damage
required_evidence: Acute onset (days to weeks) of severe dyspnea and hypoxemic respiratory failure
Arterial blood gas on room air showing marked hypoxemia (low PaO2 and SaO2) with respiratory alkalosis early (low PaCO2) and possible later mixed/respiratory acidosis if fatigue
No evidence of cardiogenic pulmonary edema (clinical assessment and/or imaging if available)
Exclusion of alternative causes of diffuse alveolar damage/ARDS (infection, aspiration, pulmonary embolism, heart failure, drug/toxin exposure, connective tissue disease)
Clinical course consistent with rapidly progressive interstitial lung disease; imaging (if obtained) expected to show bilateral diffuse ground-glass opacities/consolidation

expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) - pH: Often mildly elevated (respiratory alkalosis) early; may normalize or decrease if worsening ventilation/fatigue
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) - PaCO2: Low (hyperventilation) early; may rise if impending respiratory failure
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) - PaO2: Low (significant hypoxemia), often < 60 mmHg on room air
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) - HCO3-: Normal to mildly decreased (acute respiratory alkalosis compensation)
expected_lab_results.Lactate: Normal to mildly elevated; elevated if severe hypoxia/shock
expected_lab_results.Complete blood count - WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (stress leukocytosis); marked elevation would increase concern for infection
expected_lab_results.Complete blood count - Hemoglobin: Usually normal; may be low if chronic disease/bleeding, or high if chronic hypoxemia (less typical in acute presentation)

medications: Oxygen therapy titrated to maintain SpO2 92-96% (or PaO2 60-80 mmHg)

Methylprednisolone 1-2 mg/kg/day IV (or equivalent systemic corticosteroid) after reasonable exclusion of infection; consider pulse methylprednisolone 500-1000 mg IV daily x 3 days in fulminant cases per specialist guidance
Nintedanib 150 mg PO twice daily (continue if already taking and no contraindications; reassess if hepatic injury, bleeding risk, or intolerance)
non_pharmacological: Hospital admission (ICU-level monitoring if severe hypoxemia or increased work of breathing)
Escalate respiratory support as needed: high-flow nasal cannula; noninvasive ventilation if appropriate; early intubation for refractory hypoxemia or fatigue
Lung-protective ventilation if intubated (low tidal volume 4-8 mL/kg predicted body weight; plateau pressure < 30 cm H2O; adequate PEEP)
Conservative fluid strategy unless shock; avoid volume overload
Early consultation: pulmonology/critical care; consider lung transplant evaluation if eligible and refractory disease
Diagnostic workup to exclude triggers/alternatives (cultures/viral testing as indicated, autoimmune screening, exposure history) while treating supportively
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1-2 hours after initiating oxygen/escalated support and steroids; daily inpatient reassessment with repeat ABG/oxygenation trends; outpatient pulmonology follow-up within 1-2 weeks after discharge if stabilized

must_include: Explain that AIP is a rapidly progressive inflammation/injury of the lung tissue causing low oxygen (ARDS-like picture)
Clarify that diagnosis requires excluding other causes such as infection, heart failure, clots, aspiration, or autoimmune disease
Discuss seriousness and potential need for ICU/ventilator support
Immediate goal is to support oxygenation (oxygen/HFNC/NIV/intubation if needed)
Systemic corticosteroids are often used despite limited evidence; benefits/uncertainty and monitoring for side effects
If on nintedanib: rationale for continuation vs temporary hold depending on liver tests, bleeding risk, and tolerance
Elicit and address fears about breathing support/intubation and prognosis
Review penicillin allergy and how it affects antibiotic choices if infection becomes suspected
Discuss warning signs requiring urgent attention (worsening breathlessness, confusion, cyanosis, chest pain)
Avoid smoking and respiratory irritants; minimize exposure to dust/fumes
Vaccination and infection-prevention counseling (hand hygiene, prompt evaluation for fever/respiratory infections)
Medication adherence and monitoring (report diarrhea, bleeding, bruising, or jaundice on nintedanib; steroid side effects such as hyperglycemia, mood changes)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for acute interstitial pneumonia (AIP): rapid onset dyspnea/cough, fever, sputum/hemoptysis, chest pain, orthopnea/PND, aspiration risk, sick contacts, recent travel, occupational/environmental exposures, vaping/smoking, new meds/drugs, autoimmune symptoms, thromboembolism risk, baseline lung disease and trajectory, and current oxygen needs.

Order initial evaluation to support diagnosis and exclude mimics: CBC, CMP (including LFTs), CRP/ESR, ABG/VBG, lactate as indicated, BNP/troponin if cardiac concern, coagulation panel, blood cultures if febrile, sputum culture if productive, viral PCR (e.g., influenza/RSV/SARS-CoV-2), procalcitonin (optional), autoimmune screen if suggested (ANA, RF/CCP, myositis panel) and consider D-dimer/CTA pathway if PE risk.

Interpret available results to assess severity (hypoxemia/ARDS physiology), look for infection/cardiac/renal/hepatic abnormalities, and identify contraindications/risks relevant to steroids and antifibrotics (e.g., elevated LFTs on nintedanib).

Make a working diagnosis of AIP only after explicitly considering and attempting to exclude key alternative causes of acute hypoxemic respiratory failure (infection, cardiogenic pulmonary edema, PE, aspiration, acute eosinophilic pneumonia, diffuse alveolar hemorrhage, autoimmune/vasculitis, drug-induced lung injury).

Perform required safety check before any prescribing; confirm penicillin allergy details (reaction type, severity, timing) and document implications for empiric antibiotics if infection becomes suspected.

Check for clinically relevant drug-drug interactions prior to prescribing (e.g., anticoagulants/antiplatelets with nintedanib bleeding risk; CYP/P-gp considerations; interactions with steroid-related meds).

Initiate treatment plan appropriate for L1 AIP: immediate oxygenation support escalation plan (O2/HFNC/NIV/intubation thresholds), consider systemic corticosteroids after weighing risks/benefits and ruling out uncontrolled infection, and address nintedanib continuation vs temporary hold based on LFTs, bleeding risk, and tolerance.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (ARDS-like, exclusion of other causes, seriousness/ICU possibility), explain treatment (oxygen support, steroids with limited evidence and monitoring, nintedanib decision factors), address concerns (intubation fears/prognosis, penicillin allergy implications, warning signs), and lifestyle/prevention counseling (avoid irritants/smoking, vaccines/infection prevention, adherence and side-effect monitoring).

Arrange appropriate follow-up based on severity: urgent reassessment/ICU if unstable; otherwise close follow-up within 24–72 hours or per inpatient course, with repeat oxygen assessment and labs (especially LFTs if on nintedanib; glucose if on steroids).
Close encounter after ensuring patient understands plan, red flags, and next steps.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Prader-Willi syndrome (PWS) due to pathogenic point mutation affecting the 15q11-q13 imprinted region (loss of paternal expression)
acceptable_alternatives: Prader-Willi syndrome (genetically confirmed; molecular etiology unspecified)

required_evidence: Clinical phenotype consistent with PWS (hyperphagia/food-seeking with obesity risk; hypotonia and/or developmental delay; hypogonadism; characteristic behavioral/cognitive profile)

Genetic confirmation of PWS mechanism: abnormal methylation pattern at 15q11-q13 consistent with loss of paternal expression AND/OR identification of pathogenic point mutation in a gene within the PWS critical region with compatible imprinting effect

Exclusion of key differential when relevant: no clinical features suggesting autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy (e.g., stereotyped nocturnal hypermotor seizures) or, if suspected, normal/negative epilepsy evaluation

expected_lab_results.Total cholesterol: May be elevated; mixed dyslipidemia possible

Assess autonomic features, laterality, duration/frequency, circadian pattern, triggers (especially alcohol), and family history.
Assess comorbidities and contraindications relevant to therapy (cardiovascular disease, uncontrolled HTN, arrhythmia/heart block, pregnancy, asthma/COPD for oxygen delivery considerations).
Critical safety check prior to prescribing; confirm reported penicillin allergy and any other medication allergies.

Critical safety check prior to prescribing; evaluate interactions for triptans, verapamil, and steroid bridge (e.g., serotonergic agents, CYP3A4 inhibitors, beta-blockers, other rate-limiting agents).

Perform contraindication screening prior to prescribing: triptan vascular contraindications; verapamil conduction disease/hypotension/bradycardia; steroid risks (diabetes, infection, psychiatric history).
State diagnosis (familial cluster headache) and document reasoning and absence of urgent red flags requiring emergent workup.
Explain diagnosis milestone content, including trigeminal autonomic syndrome, miosis/partial Horner during attacks, benign but severe pain, cluster pattern, and urgent warning signs.
Initiate acute abortive therapy: high-flow oxygen and injectable/subcutaneous triptan with dosing limits and non-overuse counseling.
Initiate preventive therapy: verapamil with titration plan and required monitoring (BP/HR and ECG per protocol).
Optional/bridge therapy: short steroid taper as temporary bridge while preventive takes effect; reinforce not long-term.
Explain treatment milestone content and address concerns: side effects/stop rules, CV screening for triptan, penicillin allergy reassurance.
Lifestyle education milestone: avoid alcohol during cluster, sleep regularity, triggers, headache diary, and when to seek urgent reassessment.
Arrange follow-up for verapamil titration/ECG monitoring and reassessment of response; provide earlier review if worsening or red flags.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent elicits key cluster headache features: strictly unilateral severe orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain with ipsilateral autonomic symptoms (lacrimation, conjunctival injection, rhinorrhea, ptosis/miosis) and/or restlessness, lasting ~15–180 minutes, occurring up to multiple times per day in clusters.

Agent explicitly screens for secondary headache red flags and states urgent warning signs: thunderclap onset, fever/neck stiffness, new persistent neurologic deficits, persistent Horner syndrome between attacks, change in pattern, or continuous autonomic signs.

Agent recognizes and explains that miosis during attacks can occur as partial Horner syndrome due to autonomic involvement and that persistent Horner between attacks warrants urgent evaluation (e.g., carotid dissection).
Agent provides a two-part treatment plan: acute abortive therapy (high-flow oxygen and injectable/subcutaneous triptan) plus preventive therapy with verapamil.
Agent explains oxygen use (high-flow via non-rebreather at onset; practical instructions) and sumatriptan use with maximum daily dose limits and advice to avoid medication overuse.
Before prescribing any medication, agent completes all required critical safety checks: allergy_check, contraindication_check, and drug_interaction_check; failure to do so is a critical fail.

Agent screens for cardiovascular disease and uncontrolled hypertension before triptan prescribing and avoids triptans if contraindications are present (e.g., ischemic heart disease, prior stroke/TIA, peripheral vascular disease, uncontrolled HTN).

Agent addresses verapamil safety: checks for bradycardia, hypotension, heart block, and interacting rate-limiting drugs; counsels on BP/HR monitoring and ECG monitoring for PR prolongation/heart block per local protocol during titration.
Agent states steroid taper is a short-term bridge only and counsels on key adverse effects (mood changes, insomnia, hyperglycemia, infection risk) and when to seek care.

Agent discusses common side effects and stop/seek-care triggers: chest pain/shortness of breath after triptan; dizziness/syncope/bradycardia with verapamil; severe mood changes, severe hyperglycemia symptoms, or infection signs with prednisone.
Agent confirms penicillin allergy and reassures proposed therapies are not penicillins, while still checking for other relevant allergies (e.g., sulfa, latex).

Agent provides lifestyle counseling: avoid alcohol during active clusters, maintain regular sleep, identify/manage triggers, use a headache diary, and seek reassessment if pattern changes or autonomic signs become continuous.
Agent communicates empathetically, validates severity of pain, uses clear actionable instructions, and checks patient understanding and agreement with the plan.
Agent schedules timely follow-up for verapamil titration/monitoring and provides explicit return precautions.

general_concerns: Worries that most new symptoms mean cancer and wants a scan right away to 'be safe'.
Believes antibiotics help most illnesses, including colds and 'inflammation,' and may ask for them preemptively.
Thinks high blood pressure or diabetes would always cause obvious symptoms, so screening feels unnecessary unless she feels sick.
Assumes that if a test comes back normal once, the problem is ruled out permanently.
Believes stress is the main cause of most medical problems and that treating stress alone should fix physical symptoms.
economic_concerns: Concerned about missing work shifts and losing pay if tests or follow-ups require time off.
Worries that recommended tests will be expensive and prefers 'the one test that checks everything.'
May ration over-the-counter medications to save money or use leftover prescriptions from family members.
Prefers treatments that provide quick relief to avoid repeat visits and additional copays.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 47-year-old woman who is anxious and talks in a tangential, story-like way. Start with: “I’ve had this nagging cough for weeks and I’m just not getting better.” Early on, volunteer: cough ~3 weeks, more tired, a little short of breath on stairs, sometimes small mucus. Mention you think it’s “just allergies” (itchy eyes/sneezing some mornings) and wonder if heartburn/acid reflux is causing it. Say you tried leftover antibiotics for 2 days and expected it to fix things; you also used an old inhaler and it sort of helps.

Only if asked, disclose: low-grade evening fevers, night sweats, unintentional weight loss, chest tightness with coughing, cough worse at night, had a mild cold at the start, no major runny nose/sore throat now.

Do NOT reveal unless directly asked with sensitivity: couch surfing/shelters, skipping meals, occasional alcohol/substances, no HIV test in years, exposure to someone with a long-lasting cough, poor appetite, hoarseness, intermittent neck “glands,” getting winded with light chores.

If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, admit you’ve been scared it’s serious and avoided care. Emphasize worries about missing work, costs, and wanting “one test that checks everything” and quick relief.

medications: hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg PO daily
sertraline 50 mg PO daily
ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain
confounders: thiazide diuretic use (can worsen hyperglycemia and cause mild hyponatremia)
intermittent NSAID use (may worsen blood pressure control and renal perfusion)
obesity and suspected nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (mild transaminitis can complicate medication selection)
possible early chronic kidney disease based on reduced eGFR and albuminuria

acceptable_alternatives: Prediabetes (if A1c 5.7–6.4% and random glucose <200 mg/dL)
Type 1 diabetes mellitus (if marked hyperglycemia with low bicarbonate/anion-gap metabolic acidosis or clinical insulin deficiency)
Hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state (if very high glucose with dehydration and no significant acidosis)
Diabetic ketoacidosis (if hyperglycemia with low bicarbonate and clinical ketosis)
required_evidence: Hemoglobin A1c ≥ 6.5% OR random plasma glucose ≥ 200 mg/dL with symptoms of hyperglycemia
Basic metabolic panel without severe metabolic acidosis (bicarbonate not markedly low) to support outpatient initial management
Creatinine/eGFR adequate for metformin initiation (no severe renal impairment)

expected_lab_results.Serum glucose (random): Elevated; commonly ≥200 mg/dL in symptomatic diabetes
expected_lab_results.Basic metabolic panel - Sodium: May be mildly low due to hyperglycemia-related dilution (pseudohyponatremia) or normal

expected_lab_results.Basic metabolic panel - Bicarbonate (CO2: Normal or mildly decreased; not severely low (severe reduction would raise concern for DKA)
expected_lab_results.Basic metabolic panel - BUN: May be mildly elevated with dehydration or normal
expected_lab_results.Basic metabolic panel - Creatinine: Normal to mildly elevated; must be assessed for metformin eligibility

nl_assertions: Agent confirms diabetes diagnosis explicitly using accepted criteria (A1c >= 6.5%, fasting plasma glucose >= 126 mg/dL, 2-hr OGTT >= 200 mg/dL, or random glucose >= 200 mg/dL with symptoms) and states which criterion applies based on available results.

Agent distinguishes uncomplicated outpatient diabetes management from emergencies by screening for and explaining warning signs of DKA/HHS (vomiting, abdominal pain, rapid/deep breathing, confusion, severe weakness, inability to keep fluids down, marked dehydration) and escalates appropriately if present.

Before prescribing, agent performs a contraindication check (critical) that includes renal function (eGFR), metabolic acidosis/ketosis, severe hepatic disease, hypoxia/unstable heart failure, heavy alcohol use, and pregnancy/breastfeeding considerations as applicable; does not prescribe metformin if contraindicated.
Agent performs allergy and drug interaction checks before prescribing; notes that penicillin allergy does not impact metformin choice.

Agent selects metformin as first-line pharmacotherapy for most non-pregnant adults with type 2 diabetes without contraindications and provides a titration plan and administration with meals to reduce GI effects.
Agent explains common metformin adverse effects (GI upset/diarrhea) and provides clear guidance on management and when to seek help.

Agent explains rare lactic acidosis risk, the importance of kidney function monitoring, and advises holding metformin during severe illness/dehydration and around iodinated contrast per local protocol.
Agent elicits patient goals/preferences and barriers (cost, adherence, side effects) and incorporates them into the plan (e.g., generic options, dosing simplification, follow-up plan).
Agent provides lifestyle education: diet and physical activity can meaningfully lower A1c and reduce complications; offers realistic stepwise goals and weight loss targets if applicable.
Agent recommends or arranges diabetes self-management education and nutrition referral if available.
Agent provides explicit return precautions aligned with DKA/HHS and dehydration concerns (vomiting, abdominal pain, rapid breathing, confusion, severe weakness, inability to keep fluids down).
Agent schedules follow-up within an appropriate timeframe (typically 2–4 weeks for medication tolerance and early control; A1c reassessment around 3 months) and indicates what will be monitored.
